name: SLB N.V.

profile.description: SLB N.V. (formerly Schlumberger Limited) is the world's largest oilfield services company, operating through four divisions: Digital & Integration, Reservoir Performance, Well Construction, and Production Systems. The company provides technology-driven solutions across the energy lifecycle including drilling, completion, production, carbon management, and digital platforms. Founded in 1926, headquartered in Houston, Texas, with 109,000 employees operating in over 100 countries.

innovation.label: STRONG
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): SLB completed the $7.8B ChampionX acquisition in 2024, adding production chemicals and artificial lift capabilities, and rebranded from Schlumberger to SLB N.V. in October 2025 to signal its evolution from oilfield services to energy technology [1][3]. The company launched the Lumi AI platform and expanded the DELFI digital platform, representing a meaningful strategic shift toward data-driven energy solutions. AI Deployment (2/2): SLB's DELFI cloud platform and Lumi AI engine deploy artificial intelligence for autonomous drilling guidance, real-time subsurface interpretation, and reservoir optimization, with Rakesh Jaggi leading a dedicated Digital division that delivers measurable efficiency gains for E&P customers [3]. The AI tools interpret complex real-time subsurface data to autonomously guide drill bits through productive reservoir zones. Market Share (1/2): SLB is the largest oilfield services company globally with $35.9B in annual revenue, maintaining category leadership across wireline, drilling fluids, and subsea systems through OneSubsea, though organic revenue declined 7% in Q1 2026 when excluding the ChampionX acquisition [1]. Product Pipeline (1/2): SLB's pipeline includes expanded DELFI and Lumi AI capabilities, carbon management technology through SLB Capturi, and next-generation subsea production systems, though specific launch dates and revenue targets for individual products are not detailed [3]. R&D Intensity (1/1): R&D spending is at the top of the oilfield services sector, supporting the four-division technology portfolio and digital/AI platform development.

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (3/4): SLB's stated purpose of 'driving energy innovation for a balanced planet' is specific and thoughtful, reflecting genuine intentionality about the company's role in the energy transition beyond traditional oilfield services [3][4]. CEO Olivier Le Peuch consistently references this purpose in strategic decisions, including the expansion into carbon management and the October 2025 rebranding. The purpose differentiates from peers and shapes actual business decisions. However, it has not yet been validated by decades of consistent execution through crises. Community Impact (1/2): SLB maintains community investment programs across its 100+ country footprint and has integrated sustainability into its materiality framework, though specific charitable giving dollar amounts and program impact metrics are not prominently disclosed in public reporting [4]. Employee Retention (1/2): With 109,000 employees, SLB maintains the largest workforce in oilfield services. Le Peuch's tenure since August 2019 (6.75 years) provides leadership stability, and the company fosters a culture emphasizing innovation and diversity, though the cyclical nature of the industry creates periodic workforce adjustments. Insider Buying (1/2): Insider ownership at 0.22% is modest for a $79B company, and no significant net insider buying was identified in recent quarters [5].

infrastructure.label: STRONG
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on available metrics. Q1 2026 revenue of $8.72B grew 3% YoY (but declined 7% organically excluding the $838M ChampionX contribution), with GAAP EPS of $0.50 declining 14% YoY [1]. Full-year 2025 revenue was $35.71B, down 2% from 2024, reflecting Middle East disruptions and international activity softness. D/E of 42.5% is well below the 70% target, reflecting a conservative balance sheet with $3.5B cash against $11.6B debt. ROE of 14.1% approaches the 15% target. Forward P/E of 15.9 is attractive. Profit margin of 9.3% is below the 20% target but above oilfield services peers. Dividend metrics are solid: 40+ consecutive years paid, 4 years of consecutive growth post-2020 cut, and a manageable 50.7% payout ratio, though the 5-year dividend CAGR of 5.4% is slightly below the 6% target [1][5].

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (7/9): SLB's core business — physical oilfield services including drilling, completions, subsea production, and wireline operations — requires heavy equipment and specialized crews operating in extreme environments that AI cannot substitute [1][3]. The company's operations involve managing physical forces thousands of feet underground and on the ocean floor, requiring hardware, engineering expertise, and on-site execution. AI enhances rather than threatens SLB's business by improving drilling efficiency, reservoir characterization, and production optimization through platforms like DELFI and Lumi. No analysts have identified AI substitution risk for oilfield services operations. The modest concern is that AI-driven drilling efficiency could reduce the total number of service hours per well. AI Opportunity (1/1): SLB generates meaningful revenue from AI and digital solutions through its Digital & Integration division, which contributed approximately $4B in 2025 revenue. The DELFI platform and Lumi AI engine are commercial products sold to E&P customers, with Rakesh Jaggi leading a dedicated division focused on digital and AI revenue growth [3].

infinite_game.summary: SLB under Olivier Le Peuch demonstrates stronger infinite game characteristics than most oilfield services peers. The rebranding from Schlumberger to SLB, the 'driving energy innovation for a balanced planet' purpose, and the strategic expansion into carbon management and digital platforms all signal a company thinking beyond the current oil cycle. Le Peuch has articulated a genuine vision of SLB as an energy technology company, not just an oilfield services provider, and has backed it with capital allocation decisions including the ChampionX acquisition and SLB Capturi carbon capture business.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): SLB's purpose — 'driving energy innovation for a balanced planet' — is a genuine cause that connects the company's work to a larger vision of the energy transition [3]. Le Peuch consistently references this purpose in strategic decisions, and the October 2025 rebranding from Schlumberger to SLB was explicitly positioned as reflecting the company's evolution beyond traditional oilfield services. The expansion into carbon management (SLB Capturi) and digital energy platforms demonstrates the purpose shaping actual business strategy. However, SLB has not yet been tested through a crisis where the Just Cause conflicted with near-term profitability — the energy transition investments are still being proven.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): Le Peuch made courageous decisions including the $7.8B ChampionX acquisition to diversify beyond traditional services, the expansion into carbon capture technology through SLB Capturi, and the company rebranding — all of which required spending capital and organizational energy on long-term positioning rather than near-term returns [1][3]. However, these moves were made from a position of strength ($3B+ annual FCF) rather than true sacrifice, and the 2020 dividend cut from $2.00 to $0.50 (75% reduction), while arguably necessary, prioritized capital preservation over stakeholder commitment.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): SLB maintains the largest workforce in oilfield services at 109,000 employees with operations in 100+ countries, requiring significant decentralized decision-making [3]. Le Peuch's leadership emphasizes innovation and diversity, and the company's four-division structure empowers division presidents with significant operational authority. However, the cyclical nature of oilfield services creates periodic workforce reductions that undermine psychological safety — SLB cut thousands of positions during the 2020 downturn. The sheer scale of the organization makes uniform trust culture difficult to maintain.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): SLB has demonstrated meaningful strategic evolution — expanding from pure oilfield services into digital energy platforms (DELFI, Lumi), carbon management (SLB Capturi), and production chemicals (ChampionX acquisition), and rebranding from Schlumberger to SLB to signal the transformation [1][3]. These represent genuine adaptation to the energy transition. However, the core business remains fundamentally oil and gas services, and the new energy and digital businesses are still a small fraction of total revenue — this is evolutionary flexibility, not the transformative reinvention of an Apple or NVDA.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): SLB's competitive language under Le Peuch focuses on innovation and technology leadership rather than defeating Halliburton or Baker Hughes [3]. The company positions itself as redefining the industry category — from oilfield services to energy technology — rather than fighting for share within the existing one. Management commentary at CERAWeek and investor events emphasizes learning from the broader energy ecosystem, including technology companies, rather than framing competition as a zero-sum battle.

investment_thesis: SLB is the world's largest and most technologically diversified oilfield services company, with $35.9B in annual revenue, $3.0B in free cash flow, and a strategic vision under CEO Olivier Le Peuch that is transforming the company from traditional services into energy technology [1][3]. At 15.9x forward earnings, SLB trades at a reasonable premium to peers (HAL at 12.7x), justified by its superior international diversification, stronger balance sheet (42.5% D/E vs. HAL's 74.7%), and differentiated digital/AI platform revenue through DELFI and Lumi. The $7.8B ChampionX acquisition adds recurring production chemical revenue that diversifies beyond cyclical drilling activity. The 2.2% dividend yield with a 40+ year payment history and 4 consecutive years of post-2020 growth provides income support.
thesis_continued: The bear case centers on weakening organic fundamentals. Excluding the ChampionX acquisition, Q1 2026 revenue declined 7% organically, with GAAP EPS dropping 14% YoY to $0.50 — Middle East disruptions and international project delays are compressing the core business [1]. The 50.7% payout ratio is elevated for a cyclical business and limits financial flexibility if earnings decline further. Profit margin of 9.3% remains below the 20% target, and ROE of 14.1% has not reached the 15% threshold despite significant scale advantages. The energy transition strategy — carbon capture, digital platforms — requires sustained investment but generates uncertain near-term returns. With $11.6B in total debt, a prolonged downturn in international E&P spending could pressure both the balance sheet and the dividend, as demonstrated by the 75% cut in 2020 [5].
key_catalysts: International E&P spending recovery in the Middle East as geopolitical disruptions ease, unlocking pent-up demand across SLB's largest revenue region
DELFI and Lumi AI platform adoption accelerating digital services revenue growth, with the Digital & Integration division contributing an increasingly higher-margin revenue mix
ChampionX integration delivering $400M+ in targeted synergies while diversifying SLB's revenue toward recurring production chemical streams less sensitive to drilling activity cycles
key_risks: Organic revenue declined 7% in Q1 2026 excluding ChampionX, and sustained international activity weakness could drive further earnings compression from the current $0.50 quarterly EPS
Energy transition investments in SLB Capturi carbon capture and new energy businesses require sustained capital allocation with uncertain return timelines, potentially diluting near-term margins

The 75% dividend cut in 2020 (from $2.00 to $0.50) demonstrates that the payout is not recession-proof — the current 50.7% payout ratio leaves limited buffer if oilfield services spending enters a prolonged downcycle

SLB Full-Year 2025 Earnings Release — SEC 8-K Filing, January 2026
